title: Cat Eye Popping Out (Proptosis): Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your cat's eye is bulging or out of its socket, this is a critical emergency. Read our guide on immediate steps and how to help your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is Proptosis?

Proptosis is a serious medical condition where the feline eyeball is displaced forward, protruding from the bony eye socket and becoming trapped by the eyelids. This condition typically occurs due to severe blunt-force trauma to the head, such as a fall, a vehicle accident, or a fight with another animal.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. Proptosis is a high-priority veterinary emergency. It is an extremely painful condition that puts the cat at immediate risk of permanent blindness, nerve damage, or loss of the eye itself. If you notice your cat's eye is bulging or appears to be outside of the lid, you must seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the clinic, taking a quick photo of the eye can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary team. A clear image allows the vet to assess the degree of swelling, the color of the tissues, and whether the cornea is staying moist. This information helps the hospital prioritize your cat's arrival and prepare the necessary surgical or stabilization equipment in advance.

faqs.question: Can I push the eye back into the socket myself?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to push the eye back into the socket. Doing so can cause permanent damage to the optic nerve, blood vessels, and delicate internal structures of the eye.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the eye moist. If possible, gently apply a sterile saline solution or a water-based lubricant to a clean piece of gauze and hold it lightly over the eye to prevent the surface from drying out.

faqs.question: Will my cat lose their eye?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends on the severity of the trauma and how quickly treatment is started. While vets strive to save the eye, in cases of severe damage, surgical removal (enucleation) may be necessary to eliminate pain.
